title: Asian Virus Casts Shadow Over Supply Chain: SARS Impact on Asia/Pacific Semiconductor and Electronics Manufacturing
author: Russ Craig; Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Aberdeen Group analyst report (Russ Craig and Peter Kastner) excerpted by PlanetAnalog/EBN on April 16, 2003, examining the potential impact of the SARS epidemic on the PRC- and Taiwan-centered global electronics supply chain. The authors quantify Asia/Pacific's 37% share of 2002 semiconductor consumption ($52B), document early SARS clusters in Guangdong, Shenzhen, Guangzhou, Hong Kong, Singapore, and Taiwan, and assess the vulnerability of dependable component supply — particularly for no-second-source items like PRC-made AC/DC power supplies — should quarantines extend. The report ends with a prescient warning that investor confidence in the PRC will take years to rebuild given government opacity about the outbreak.
importance_rationale: First widely-syndicated Western analyst report quantifying SARS's supply-chain exposure for the global semiconductor and PC industry; established Aberdeen's voice as a go-to source across 20+ outlets (NYT, WSJ, CNBC, USA Today) that followed in April-May 2003 (see quotes CSV seqs 107-141 for the syndication trail).
relevance_rationale: The framing of pandemic-driven supply shock — single-source component dependence, PRC opacity, and the multi-year investor trust cost — is the same playbook applied to COVID-19 (2020-2022) and the Taiwan-strait semiconductor concentration debate still live in 2026.
prescience_rationale: Called out the 'no-second-source' PRC component risk (AC/DC power supplies, low-level assemblies) 17 years before the COVID-19 PPE, chip, and consumer-electronics shortages proved the thesis. Also predicted multi-year investor skepticism of PRC government credibility — validated by the 2020 US-China decoupling and Apple/Foxconn diversification to Vietnam and India.
